flashcards.front: What is the definition of a viable prefix?
flashcards.back: A viable prefix is any prefix of a right-sentential form that can appear on the stack of an LR parser without causing a syntax error. It represents a partially parsed input that could be completed to a valid sentence.

flashcards.front: Why are viable prefixes important for Syntax Directed Definitions in LR parsing?
flashcards.back: Viable prefixes determine when semantic actions from SDDs can safely execute. They ensure attributes are computed only when the parser has recognized a complete grammatical construct, preventing incorrect attribute values from premature computation.

flashcards.front: What property must SDDs have to work correctly with LR parsers and viable prefixes?
flashcards.back: SDDs must be L-attributed. This means inherited attributes can only depend on attributes from left siblings or parents, allowing computation during left-to-right parsing that matches LR parser operation with viable prefixes.

flashcards.front: How do viable prefixes affect when reductions occur in LR parsing?
flashcards.back: Viable prefixes determine the parser states where reductions are applicable. When the parser recognizes a viable prefix corresponding to the right-hand side of a production, it can perform a reduction and execute associated semantic actions from SDDs.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between viable prefixes and parser stack contents?
flashcards.back: The contents of the LR parser stack always form a viable prefix. This invariant ensures the parser is always in a valid state, and semantic actions from SDDs execute only when the stack contains a complete viable prefix ready for reduction.
